sections.title: Computer system misuse and data manipulation prohibitions
sections.summary: This section prohibits unauthorized access, data extraction, introduction of malware, system damage, disruption, denial of access, and other malicious activities on computer systems. It applies to any person who uses computer resources without authorization or causes harm to computer systems. The section also establishes liability for damages caused by such activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines prohibited activities that could impact data transfer security, particularly where data manipulation or system damage occurs across borders. It requires safeguards against unauthorized data access and manipulation during cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Prohibits downloading, copying or extracting data from computer systems
Prohibits introducing computer contaminants or viruses into computer systems
Prohibits damaging computer systems, data or programs
Prohibits disrupting computer systems or networks
Prohibits denying authorized access to computer systems
Prohibits providing assistance to unauthorized access to computer systems
Prohibits charging services to another person through tampering with computer systems
Prohibits destroying, deleting or altering information in computer resources
Prohibits stealing, concealing, destroying or altering computer source code

sections.summary: This section outlines various computer-related offences including identity theft, cyber terrorism, privacy violations, and cheating by personation. It specifies penalties for these offences, applying to individuals and entities that commit such acts using computer resources. The section does not define specific offences but lists them for legal reference.

sections.title: Government authority to block public access to information
sections.summary: This section empowers the Central Government to block public access to information in specific circumstances, such as national security or public order. Intermediaries must comply with these blocking orders, with non-compliance resulting in penalties up to 7 years. The law applies to entities hosting or processing data electronically.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows for blocking data access across borders, which could impact data storage and transfer. Entities must comply with blocking orders to avoid severe penalties.
sections.source_quote: Section 69A(1) of the Information Technology Act, 2000

sections.heading: Protected system.
sections.title: Critical information infrastructure protection
sections.summary: This section defines critical information infrastructure as computer resources whose incapacitation or destruction would have a debilitating impact on national security, economy, public health, or safety. The appropriate government may declare such systems as protected, and unauthorized access to these systems is punishable by imprisonment up to 10 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section identifies critical infrastructure that requires special protection, which could impact cross-border data transfers involving sensitive systems. Entities must ensure compliance with protection measures.

requirements.text: Appropriate government may declare computer resources affecting critical infrastructure as protected systems
Unauthorized access to protected systems is punishable by imprisonment up to 10 years

sections.source_quote: Section 70(1) and (3) of the Information Technology Act, 2000

sections.heading: Penalty for Breach of confidentiality and privacy.
sections.title: Penalty for unauthorized disclosure of electronic records
sections.summary: Any person who accesses electronic records without consent and discloses them to others faces a penalty of up to five lakh rupees. This applies to individuals acting under the powers of the Act or regulations. The penalty is subject to other laws in force at the time.
sections.dtia_summary: Imposes a financial penalty for unauthorized disclosure of electronic records, relevant to data transfer safeguards.

requirements.text: Any person who accesses electronic records without consent and discloses them to others faces a penalty of up to five lakh rupees

sections.source_quote: 72. Penalty for Breach of confidentiality and privacy.–Save as otherwise provided in this Act or any other law for the time being in force, if any person who, in pursuance of any of the powers conferred under this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder, has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material without the consent of the person concerned discloses such electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material to any other person shall be 3[liable to penalty which may extend to five lakh rupees].

The section applies to entities that act in good faith and those involved in encryption practices, ensuring legal safeguards for cybersecurity measures.

sections.heading: Certifying Authority to follow certain procedures.–Every Certifying Authority shall,–
sections.title: Certifying Authority security and reliability requirements
sections.summary: Certifying Authorities must implement secure hardware, software, and procedures to prevent intrusion and misuse. They must ensure service reliability for intended functions, maintain secrecy and privacy of electronic signatures, store all issued certificates, publish practices and certificate status, and comply with additional standards specified by regulations. These requirements apply to all Certifying Authorities under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Certifying Authorities must maintain robust security measures and transparency in certificate management, directly affecting the reliability of electronic signatures used in cross-border data transfers.

These obligations apply to all Certifying Authorities under the Act.

sections.title: Application process for electronic signature certificates
sections.summary: Any person may apply to a Certifying Authority for an electronic signature certificate with a fee not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees. The Certifying Authority may grant or reject applications after considering the statement and making inquiries, but must provide a reasonable opportunity to show cause against rejection. This applies to all applicants seeking electronic signature certificates.

sections.heading: Revocation of Digital Signature Certificate.–(1) A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital
sections.title: Revocation of Digital Signature Certificates
sections.summary: Certifying Authorities may revoke digital signature certificates under specific conditions: subscriber request, subscriber death, firm/company dissolution, false material facts, unsatisfied requirements, compromised security systems, or subscriber insolvency/death. Revocation must provide the subscriber a hearing opportunity and notify the subscriber immediately upon revocation. These obligations apply to all Certifying Authorities under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides conditions for revoking digital signature certificates, including security and reliability checks, critical for assessing data transfer risks when certificates are compromised.

requirements.text: A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate issued by it where the subscriber or any other person authorised by him makes a request to that effect
A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate issued by it upon the death of the subscriber
A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate issued by it upon the dissolution of the firm or winding up of the company where the subscriber is a firm or a company

A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate which has been issued by it at any time if it is of opinion that a material fact represented in the Digital Signature Certificate is false or has been concealed

A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate which has been issued by it at any time if it is of opinion that a requirement for issuance of the Digital Signature Certificate was not satisfied

A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate which has been issued by it at any time if it is of opinion that the Certifying Authority's private key or security system was compromised in a manner materially affecting the Digital Signature Certificate's reliability

A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate which has been issued by it at any time if it is of opinion that the subscriber has been declared insolvent or dead or where a subscriber is a firm or a company, which has been dissolved, wound-up or otherwise ceased to exist
A Digital Signature Certificate shall not be revoked unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard in the matter
On revocation of a Digital Signature Certificate under this section, the Certifying Authority shall communicate the same to the subscriber

sections.source_quote: 38. Revocation of Digital Signature Certificate.–(1) A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate issued by it– (a) where the subscriber or any other person authorised by him makes a request to that effect; or (b) upon the death of the subscriber; or (c) upon the dissolution of the firm or winding up of the company where the subscriber is a firm or a company. (2) Subject to the provisions of sub-section (3) and without prejudice to the provisions of sub-section (1), a Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate which has been issued by it at any time, if it is of opinion that– (a) a material fact represented in the Digital Signature Certificate is false or has been concealed; (b) a requirement for issuance of the Digital Signature Certificate was not satisfied; (c) the Certifying Authority's private key or security system was compromised in a manner materially affecting the Digital Signature Certificate's reliability; (d) the subscriber has been declared insolvent or dead or where a subscriber is a firm or a company, which has been dissolved, wound-up or otherwise ceased to exist. (3) A Digital Signature Certificate shall not be revoked unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard in the matter. (4) On revocation of a Digital Signature Certificate under this section, the Certifying Authority shall communicate the same to the subscriber.

sections.title: Private key control and compromise notification
sections.summary: Subscribers must exercise reasonable care to retain control of their private keys and prevent disclosure. sections.dtia_summary: Mandates immediate notification of private key compromise, critical for maintaining data security during cross-border electronic signature transfers.

requirements.text: Every subscriber shall exercise reasonable care to retain control of the private key corresponding to the public key listed in his Digital Signature Certificate and take all steps to prevent its disclosure

sections.source_quote: 42. Prohibits providing assistance to unauthorized access to computer systems
Prohibits charging services to another person through tampering with computer systems
Prohibits destroying, deleting or altering information in computer resources
Prohibits stealing, concealing, destroying or altering computer source code

sections.title: Penalties for publishing obscene or sexually explicit material online
sections.summary: This section imposes penalties for publishing or transmitting obscene material in electronic form, including sexually explicit content. It specifies imprisonment terms and fines for first and subsequent convictions, with special provisions for children's material. The law excludes materials justified for public good or heritage/religious purposes. The definition of 'children' includes persons under 18 years.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses content-related data sharing risks, particularly for child sexual abuse material. It requires careful handling of sensitive data to avoid penalties under this provision.

requirements.text: Whoever publishes or transmits obscene material in electronic form shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to 5 lakhs
Whoever publishes or transmits sexually explicit material shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine up to 10 lakhs
Whoever publishes or transmits material depicting children in sexually explicit acts shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine up to 10 lakhs

Whoever creates, collects, or distributes material depicting children in obscene or sexually explicit manner shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine up to 10 lakhs
Whoever cultivates or induces children for online sexual relationships shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine up to 10 lakhs

sections.title: e-authentication techniques for digital signatures
sections.summary: This section details the e-authentication techniques and procedures for creating and accessing subscriber's signature keys. It specifies that authentication must use e-authentication, hash, and asymmetric crypto system techniques, involve trusted third parties, and comply with digital signature guidelines issued by the Controller.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes technical requirements for digital signatures, including trusted third parties and cryptographic standards, which impact cross-border data transfers involving digital signatures.

requirements.text: e-authentication technique using Aadhaar or other e-KYC services for authentication of electronic records by e-authentication technique

Digital Signature Certificate issuance by Certifying Authority based on e-authentication, particulars specified in Form C of Schedule IV of the Information Technology (Certifying Authorities) Rules, 2000, digitally signed verified information from Aadhaar or other e-KYC services and electronic consent of Digital Signature Certificate applicant
The security procedure for creating the subscriber's key pair shall be in accordance with the e-authentication guidelines issued by the Controller
The manner and requirements for e-authentication shall be as issued by the Controller from time to time

The standards referred to in Rule 6 of the Information Technology (Certifying Authorities) Rules, 2000 shall be complied with, in so far as they relate to the certification function of public key of Digital Signature Certificate applicant

The manner in which information is authenticated by means of digital signature shall comply with the manner and standards specified in Rules 3 to 12 of the Digital Signature (End entity) Rules, 2015 in so far as they relate to the creation, storage, and verification of Digital Signature
e-authentication technique and procedure for creating and accessing subscriber's signature key facilitated by trusted third party
Identity verification of Digital Signature Certificate applicant shall be in accordance with the Identity Verification Guidelines issued by Controller from time-to-time
The requirement to operate as trusted third party shall be specified under e-authentication guidelines issued by the Controller
A trusted third party shall facilitate Identity verification of Digital Signature Certificate applicant
A trusted third party shall establish secure storage for subscriber to have sole control for creation and subsequent usage of subscriber's signature key by sole authentication of subscriber
A trusted third party shall facilitate key pair-generation, secure storage of subscriber's signature key and facilitate signature creation functions

A trusted third party shall facilitate the submission of DSC application form and certificate signing request to the Certifying Authority for issuing a Digital Signature Certificate to the DSC applicant
A trusted third party shall facilitate revocation of Digital Signature Certificate and destruction of subscriber's signature key
The manner and requirements for authentication and storage of keys shall be as issued by the Controller from time to time under e-authentication guidelines

sections.source_quote: 6. Ins. by S.O. 1119(E), dated 1-3-2019. ... e-authentication technique using Aadhaar or other e-KYC services ... The security procedure for creating the subscriber's key pair shall be in accordance with the e-authentication guidelines issued by the Controller.

sections.heading: Constitution of Advisory Committee.–(1) The Central Government shall, as soon as may be
sections.title: Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee establishment
sections.summary: This section mandates the Central Government to establish a Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee that includes both official and non-official members representing stakeholders. The Committee advises the Central Government on rules and regulations related to the Act and the Controller in framing regulations. These provisions apply to the Central Government and the Controller, with the Committee serving as a consultative body for regulatory development.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a governance structure for regulatory development that could influence data transfer frameworks and compliance mechanisms.

requirements.text: Central Government shall constitute Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee
Committee shall consist of Chairperson and other official and non-official members
Committee shall advise Central Government on rules and regulations
Committee shall advise Controller in framing regulations

sections.source_quote: The Central Government shall, as soon as may be after the commencement of this Act, constitute a Committee called the Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee

sections.title: Cyber Appellate Tribunal designation
sections.summary: This section is a substitutional provision that updates the name of the Cyber Appellate Tribunal from the previous designation to the current one, effective from May 26, 2017. It does not contain substantive obligations or requirements but serves as a technical amendment to the tribunal's name.

sections.heading: Power of Controller to make regulations.–(1) The Controller may, after consultation with the
sections.title: Controller's regulatory powers for certification authorities
sections.summary: This section grants the Controller authority to make regulations with Central Government approval for implementing the Act, particularly concerning certification authorities. The regulations may specify data base maintenance requirements, foreign certification authority recognition conditions, licensing terms, and procedures for disclosing information. These provisions apply to certification authorities and the Controller, with the regulations impacting cross-border data transfers involving electronic signatures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes regulatory frameworks for certification authorities that could impact cross-border data transfers involving electronic signatures and authentication mechanisms.

requirements.text: Controller may make regulations consistent with this Act
Regulations may provide for data base maintenance of disclosure records
Regulations may specify conditions for recognising foreign certification authorities
Regulations may define terms and conditions for licensing
Regulations may specify other standards for certification authorities
Regulations may define manner for disclosing specified matters
Regulations may specify statement details for applications
Regulations may specify manner for communicating private key compromise

sections.source_quote: The Controller may, after consultation with the Cyber Regulations Advisory Committee and with the previous approval of the Central Government, by notification in the Official Gazette, make regulations consistent with this Act

sections.heading: Power of State Government to make rules.–(1) The State Government may, by notification in
sections.title: State-level regulatory rules for electronic transactions
sections.summary: This section empowers State Governments to create rules for implementing the Act, particularly concerning electronic transaction procedures. The rules may specify electronic forms for filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment. These provisions apply to State Governments and entities operating under the Act, with the rules impacting electronic transaction processes and data storage.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows state-level rules that could impact data storage and processing for electronic transactions across borders.

requirements.text: State Government may make rules to carry out provisions of this Act
Rules may specify electronic form for filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment
Rules may specify procedures for matters specified in sub-section (2) of section 6

sections.source_quote: The State Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, make rules to carry out the provisions of this Act
